_meta.authoringNotes: Diagnostic criteria from Institute of Medicine 2015 (SEID criteria, NAS report) and International Consensus Criteria 2011 (Carruthers et al. J Intern Med 2011 PMID 21777306). Epidemiology from CDC 2024 ME/CFS data sheet and Lim EJ et al. J Transl Med 2020 (PMID 32093722) meta-analysis of prevalence. Pathophysiology per Komaroff AL. JAMA 2019 (PMID 31251366) and Naviaux RK et al. PNAS 2016 (PMID 27573827). Management per NICE NG206 (2021/2024 update — note removal of graded exercise therapy as recommended treatment). Post-COVID overlap per Davis HE et al. Nat Rev Microbiol 2023 (PMID 36639607). Pacing evidence per Bavinton J et al. J R Soc Med 2019. Low-dose naltrexone per Bolton MJ et al. BMJ Case Rep 2020. Pyridostigmine for orthostatic intolerance per Dr Systrom & Massachusetts General Hospital work. PACE trial criticism per Wilshire CE et al. Fatigue 2018.

h1Title: Chronic Fatigue Syndrome: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Chronic fatigue syndrome — formally myalgic encephalomyelitis / chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS) — is a chronic, complex, multisystem disease characterized by profound, disabling fatigue that does not improve with rest and is made dramatically worse by physical, cognitive, or emotional exertion. Post-exertional malaise (PEM) — the worsening of symptoms hours to days after even modest activity — is the cardinal feature that separates ME/CFS from other forms of chronic tiredness. The US Institute of Medicine 2015 SEID criteria require substantial reduction in pre-illness activity for at least 6 months, post-exertional malaise, unrefreshing sleep, and either cognitive impairment or orthostatic intolerance. CDC 2024 estimates 836,000-3.3 million Americans live with ME/CFS, with global prevalence of 0.4-2.5%; women are affected 2-4 times more often than men, and onset typically follows a viral or other acute illness. The COVID-19 pandemic has substantially increased the number of people meeting ME/CFS criteria through long COVID. Management focuses on pacing (energy management) to avoid PEM crashes, symptomatic treatment of orthostatic intolerance, sleep, pain, and cognitive symptoms, and individualized rehabilitation; graded exercise therapy is no longer recommended (NICE NG206, 2021/2024) because it can cause sustained worsening. No biomarker or curative treatment exists; only modest spontaneous recovery rates (5-30%) are seen, although function can improve substantially with rigorous symptom management.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 0.4-2.5% of adults globally; CDC 2024 estimates 836,000-3.3 million Americans with ME/CFS
keyStats.demographics: Women affected 2-4 times more than men; peak onset ages 20-50, but children and adolescents are affected and older adults can develop the condition
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at diagnosis 30-50; pediatric onset accounts for 0.1-0.5% of all cases
keyStats.globalCases: Global burden estimated 17-24 million people; long COVID has increased prevalence substantially since 2020

definition: Chronic fatigue syndrome — also called myalgic encephalomyelitis / chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS), systemic exertion intolerance disease (SEID), and post-viral fatigue syndrome — is a chronic, complex, multisystem disease characterized by profound disabling fatigue and post-exertional malaise. ICD-10 codes include G93.32 (myalgic encephalomyelitis) and R53.82 (chronic fatigue, unspecified); G93.3 (postviral fatigue syndrome) and U09.9 (post-COVID condition unspecified) also apply for specific subgroups. The Institute of Medicine 2015 report defined the criteria as: substantial reduction or impairment in the ability to engage in pre-illness levels of activity for more than 6 months, accompanied by fatigue that is profound, new onset, not the result of ongoing excessive exertion, and not substantially alleviated by rest; post-exertional malaise; unrefreshing sleep; AND at least one of cognitive impairment or orthostatic intolerance. Multiple alternative criteria sets exist (Fukuda 1994, Canadian Consensus Criteria 2003, International Consensus Criteria 2011), and clinicians select based on context. The pathophysiology is incompletely understood but includes evidence of mitochondrial dysfunction, autonomic dysregulation (particularly postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome and orthostatic hypotension), HPA-axis blunting, persistent immune activation, microbiome alterations, central sensitization, and microvascular dysfunction. ME/CFS is managed by primary care physicians, internal medicine, neurologists with chronic disease expertise, rheumatologists, autonomic-dysfunction specialists, and rehabilitation services. Specific ME/CFS clinics exist in the UK (NHS commissioned services), the US (Bateman Horne Center, Stanford ME/CFS Initiative, Open Medicine Foundation), and elsewhere.

typesClassification.description: Onset following a documented infection (Epstein-Barr virus, COVID-19, Lyme disease, enterovirus, parvovirus, Coxiella, atypical bacterial pneumonia). Accounts for over 70% of incident cases in recent studies.

typesClassification.type: Post-COVID ME/CFS (long COVID with ME/CFS phenotype)
typesClassification.description: Subset of long COVID patients who meet ME/CFS diagnostic criteria after SARS-CoV-2 infection. The pandemic has dramatically expanded the number of patients meeting ME/CFS criteria.
Insidious onset without identifiable infectious or traumatic trigger. Comprises a smaller proportion of cases but presents the same clinical picture.
Patients can typically work or attend school but with reduced overall function (50-80% of normal); social and recreational activities are markedly reduced. Pacing maintains function.

Patients are typically unable to maintain full-time work or school. They are mainly housebound with frequent rest, although they can manage essential household tasks. Significantly impaired quality of life.

Patients are mostly or entirely bed-bound. Severe ME/CFS includes those who require wheelchair use; very severe includes patients who cannot tolerate light, sound, or any stimulus. Approximately 25% of ME/CFS patients fall in these severe categories.

Children and adolescents with ME/CFS face educational disruption, social isolation, and developmental impact. Diagnostic criteria modified for younger patients (Carruthers Pediatric Diagnostic Criteria 2007).

primarySymptoms: Substantial reduction in pre-illness activity (work, school, social, recreational) lasting more than 6 months, with fatigue that is profound, new onset, and not substantially alleviated by rest.

Post-exertional malaise (PEM): worsening of symptoms after physical, cognitive, or emotional exertion, often delayed by 12-72 hours and lasting hours to weeks; the cardinal symptom required for diagnosis.
Unrefreshing sleep — waking exhausted despite apparently adequate sleep duration; difficulty staying asleep and disturbed sleep architecture are common.
Cognitive impairment ('brain fog') with difficulties in attention, concentration, working memory, word finding, and processing speed.

Orthostatic intolerance — symptoms (lightheadedness, palpitations, fatigue) worse on standing or sitting upright; postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) and orthostatic hypotension are common.
Widespread musculoskeletal pain (myalgia and arthralgia) without inflammation; often fluctuating.
Persistent headaches of new pattern (often migraine-like) since illness onset.
Sore throat and tender cervical or axillary lymphadenopathy, particularly after exertion.
Light, sound, smell, and chemical sensitivity (sensory hypersensitivity).
Gastrointestinal symptoms — bloating, abdominal pain, alternating diarrhea and constipation (functional bowel symptoms).
Temperature dysregulation — feeling cold or hot inappropriately, low-grade fevers, night sweats.
Cognitive overload after social interaction or screen time.
Emotional and behavioural changes including anxiety, depression, anger, frustration (often reactive to chronic disability rather than primary).
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent unexplained fatigue lasting more than 3 months that fails to improve with rest
Sudden inability to maintain previous level of activity after a viral illness — particularly Epstein-Barr virus, COVID-19, or another infection
Cognitive difficulty that emerges or worsens after exertion or stress
Lightheadedness on standing or after meals in a previously well young or middle-aged adult
Worsening of all symptoms 12-72 hours after even modest physical or cognitive activity — the hallmark of post-exertional malaise
emergencySigns: Severe orthostatic instability with syncope, seizures, or injury — exclude pulmonary embolism, arrhythmia, or other acute cardiovascular cause
Severe weight loss with profound weakness — investigate for malignancy, eating disorder, or other systemic disease
Severe depression with suicidal ideation — common in chronic disabling illness and requires urgent mental health assessment
New focal neurological deficit, severe headache with altered consciousness, or seizure — exclude structural neurological pathology
Severe dysphagia, weight loss, or aspiration in very severe ME/CFS — requires gastroenterology and nutritional support

causes.description: Approximately 70-80% of incident ME/CFS cases begin after a documented infection. Common triggers include Epstein-Barr virus (mononucleosis), SARS-CoV-2 (the major cause since 2020), human herpesvirus 6/7, enterovirus, parvovirus B19, Borrelia burgdorferi (Lyme disease), Coxiella burnetii (Q fever), giardia, and atypical bacterial pneumonia. The pathway from infection to chronic disease is incompletely understood.

A substantial subset of patients with persistent post-COVID symptoms meet ME/CFS criteria. Long COVID has driven a major expansion in ME/CFS prevalence globally. Shared symptoms, pathophysiology (autonomic dysfunction, immune dysregulation, mitochondrial dysfunction), and management strategies suggest considerable overlap.

Up to 70% of ME/CFS patients have demonstrable orthostatic intolerance (postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome, orthostatic hypotension, or neurally-mediated hypotension). Tilt-table testing and active-stand testing are useful diagnostic tools.

Studies show reduced ATP production, altered intracellular metabolism, and metabolic phenotype resembling 'cell danger response' (Naviaux PNAS 2016). Aerobic threshold falls and recovery from exercise is impaired on cardiopulmonary exercise testing.

Subtle changes in natural killer cell function, cytokine profiles, autoimmunity (against beta-adrenergic and muscarinic receptors), and persistent low-grade immune activation have been documented. No diagnostic biomarker has yet emerged.
PET imaging has demonstrated neuroinflammation in some patients; central sensitization is hypothesized to amplify sensory inputs producing pain, light, sound, and chemical sensitivity.
Modest familial clustering and genetic association studies suggest contribution of immune, autonomic, and metabolic gene variants. Severity may have hereditary component.

Chronic stress, trauma, and adverse early-life experiences may predispose to or worsen ME/CFS but are NOT the primary cause. The 'biopsychosocial' framing historically caused harm by minimizing the biological basis; current consensus places ME/CFS firmly in the biomedical disease category.

riskFactors.factor: Recent acute infection (especially Epstein-Barr virus or SARS-CoV-2)
riskFactors.description: Single most important risk factor. Post-EBV ME/CFS occurs in approximately 5-12% of adolescents and adults with infectious mononucleosis; post-COVID ME/CFS in approximately 2-5% of COVID-19 survivors.
Women are 2-4 times more affected, particularly in post-infectious and post-COVID forms.
Peak onset in working-age adults, although adolescents and older adults can develop the condition.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of ME/CFS or other functional disorders
riskFactors.description: Some familial aggregation has been documented. First-degree relatives of patients with ME/CFS have higher rates of the condition.

riskFactors.factor: History of autoimmune or atopic disease
riskFactors.description: Patients with prior autoimmune thyroid disease, Sjögren syndrome, or significant allergies are over-represented in ME/CFS cohorts.
Anxiety and depression frequently precede or coexist with ME/CFS but do not appear to cause it. Adverse childhood experiences are over-represented in adult ME/CFS.

riskFactors.factor: Physical or emotional stressor at illness onset
riskFactors.description: Major life stressor, surgery, trauma, or hormonal change is reported in roughly 30-50% of incident cases.

affectedDemographics: United States: CDC 2024 estimates 836,000-3.3 million Americans with ME/CFS, with prevalence rising since the COVID-19 pandemic.
United Kingdom: estimated 250,000 people; the UK NICE NG206 guideline (2021, updated 2024) is among the most prominent national resources.
Global: meta-analyses estimate 0.4-2.5% adult prevalence depending on diagnostic criteria. Higher with broader Fukuda criteria, lower with restrictive ICC criteria.
Female-to-male ratio approximately 2-4:1 across geographies and criteria.
Long COVID has increased ME/CFS prevalence by an estimated 30-50% in many high-income countries, driving renewed clinical and research attention.
Severe ME/CFS (housebound or bedbound) is estimated at 25% of all cases — these patients are often invisible to clinical services because they cannot attend appointments.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of ME/CFS is clinical, based on validated criteria and exclusion of medical conditions that could explain the symptom complex. The Institute of Medicine 2015 SEID criteria are commonly used: (1) substantial reduction or impairment in the ability to engage in pre-illness levels of activity for more than 6 months, accompanied by profound new-onset fatigue not substantially alleviated by rest; (2) post-exertional malaise; (3) unrefreshing sleep; (4) AND at least one of cognitive impairment or orthostatic intolerance. International Consensus Criteria 2011 add post-exertional neuroimmune exhaustion (PENEM) and broader symptom categories. There is no diagnostic biomarker; investigations rule out alternative explanations. Baseline workup includes complete blood count, ESR, CRP, comprehensive metabolic panel (renal and liver function, electrolytes, calcium, magnesium, phosphate), thyroid function (TSH, free T4), ferritin, B12 and folate, vitamin D, HbA1c, urinalysis, ANA, anti-CCP, rheumatoid factor, creatine kinase, IgG/IgA/IgM, EBV serology, HIV, hepatitis B and C, Lyme serology if exposure risk, and cortisol if features suggest adrenal insufficiency. Additional studies based on clinical features may include sleep study (for unrefreshing sleep), tilt-table or active-stand test (for orthostatic intolerance), 2-day cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET — can document characteristic decline in oxygen uptake on day 2), and brain MRI if focal neurology. Once medical exclusions are made and IOM criteria met, the diagnosis is established. Psychiatric assessment for depression, anxiety, and PTSD is important — these often coexist but do not by themselves account for ME/CFS. Specialist referral to an ME/CFS clinic, neurologist, or autonomic specialist is recommended for severe cases and POTS workup.

diagnosticTests.test: Comprehensive metabolic panel, CBC, ESR, CRP
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes anemia, thyroid disease, renal or hepatic disease, inflammation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 24-48 hours. Typically normal in ME/CFS, supporting the diagnosis by exclusion.

diagnosticTests.test: TSH, free T4, ferritin, vitamin B12, vitamin D, HbA1c
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes thyroid disease, iron deficiency, B12 deficiency, vitamin D deficiency, and diabetes — all commonly mimic ME/CFS
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Same blood sample; results within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes systemic lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, Sjögren syndrome
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 3-7 days. Mildly positive ANA is common and not always significant.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies orthostatic intolerance, POTS, neurally-mediated hypotension
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Tilt-table: lasts 30-45 minutes on a motorized table with continuous heart rate and blood pressure monitoring. Active-stand: 10-minute supine then 10-minute standing with serial measurements.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes sleep apnea, narcolepsy, or other sleep disorders that could explain fatigue
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Overnight study in a sleep laboratory or home study. Results within 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates characteristic ME/CFS pattern of impaired aerobic capacity and post-exertional decline on day 2
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Exercise testing to volitional exhaustion on two consecutive days. Risk of provoking severe PEM; performed only at specialized centres and with informed consent. Results within 2-4 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes treatable infections in patients with relevant exposure
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within 1-2 weeks.

treatmentOverview: There is no FDA-approved disease-modifying treatment for ME/CFS. Management combines pacing (energy management to stay within the individual's energy envelope and avoid post-exertional malaise), symptomatic treatment, treatment of comorbid conditions, and supportive care. Pacing is the cornerstone intervention: patients learn to monitor their energy expenditure with heart-rate monitors or activity diaries, divide tasks into manageable segments, plan rest before fatigue accumulates, and avoid 'push-crash' cycles. The 2021 NICE NG206 guideline (updated 2024) explicitly removed graded exercise therapy (GET) as a recommended treatment because it can cause sustained worsening in ME/CFS; the older PACE trial that supported GET has been extensively criticized. Symptomatic treatment includes: melatonin and short-term sleep aids for sleep, low-dose tricyclics (amitriptyline) for sleep and pain, gabapentin or pregabalin for neuropathic pain, NSAIDs and topical analgesics for musculoskeletal pain (with caution), low-dose naltrexone (1.5-4.5 mg nightly) for pain and immune modulation (off-label, growing evidence), pyridostigmine for orthostatic intolerance, midodrine and fludrocortisone for orthostatic hypotension and POTS, ivabradine for POTS-related tachycardia, increased fluid and salt intake for orthostatic intolerance, beta-blockers (low-dose propranolol or atenolol) for selected POTS patients, and SSRI/SNRI antidepressants only when comorbid depression is present (treating depression does not cure ME/CFS). Cognitive symptoms are managed with stimulants in selected patients, cognitive-behavioural strategies for living with chronic illness (NOT cognitive-behavioural therapy as a cure), and accommodations at work or school. Long COVID-specific interventions including immunomodulatory and antiviral strategies are being studied in trials. Severe and very severe patients require home-based care with attention to nutrition, pressure injury prevention, sensory environment, and family support. Specialist multidisciplinary clinics with experience in ME/CFS, occupational therapists trained in pacing, autonomic-dysfunction services, and disability evaluation services all support best management. Patient education and connection with reputable support communities help reduce isolation and improve coping.

medicalTreatments.name: Pacing (energy management) — heart rate-guided activity management with anaerobic threshold monitoring
medicalTreatments.description: Cornerstone non-pharmacological intervention. Patients monitor heart rate, activity, and fatigue to stay below the anaerobic threshold and avoid post-exertional malaise. Use of heart-rate monitors (target HR below 60% of max or below identified anaerobic threshold) is increasingly standard.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces frequency and severity of PEM crashes; modest functional improvement in selected patients

medicalTreatments.name: Low-dose naltrexone 1.5-4.5 mg orally at bedtime
medicalTreatments.description: Off-label opioid antagonist used at low doses for pain, fatigue, and immune modulation. Generally well-tolerated; can cause initial sleep disturbance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Moderate symptomatic improvement in 30-50% of patients in open-label studies; randomized trial evidence emerging

medicalTreatments.name: Pyridostigmine 30-60 mg orally three times daily
medicalTreatments.description: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitor that improves orthostatic tolerance in selected ME/CFS patients with documented postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome or neurally-mediated hypotension.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improvement in orthostatic symptoms in 40-60% of selected patients (Systrom et al. data from Massachusetts General Hospital)

medicalTreatments.name: Midodrine 2.5-10 mg orally three times daily
medicalTreatments.description: Alpha-1 agonist used for orthostatic hypotension and POTS. Take in the morning and through the day; avoid within 4 hours of bedtime to prevent supine hypertension.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement in 50-70% of orthostatic-symptom patients

medicalTreatments.name: Fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg orally daily
medicalTreatments.description: Mineralocorticoid for orthostatic intolerance; expands plasma volume. Monitor potassium and blood pressure; avoid in renal impairment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic relief of orthostatic symptoms in 40-60% of patients

medicalTreatments.name: Increased salt and fluid intake (3-5 g salt/day, 2-3 L fluids/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Increases plasma volume and reduces orthostatic symptoms. Cheap and well-tolerated; combined with compression stockings and avoidance of upright postural triggers.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic improvement in over 50% of POTS patients

medicalTreatments.name: Sleep medications (low-dose amitriptyline 10-25 mg, mirtazapine 7.5-30 mg, melatonin, trazodone)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for unrefreshing sleep and insomnia. Low-dose tricyclics provide both sleep and pain benefit; avoid daytime sedation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Sleep quality improvement in 50-70% of patients

medicalTreatments.name: Pain management (gabapentin 100-3600 mg/day, pregabalin 75-600 mg/day, topical NSAIDs)
medicalTreatments.description: Multimodal approach for widespread pain. Avoid long-term opioids. Address comorbid migraine with appropriate prophylaxis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) for adjustment to chronic illness
alternativeTreatments.description: CBT is no longer recommended as a curative treatment for ME/CFS per NICE NG206 (2021). Brief CBT focused on coping with chronic illness — not on the assumption that ME/CFS is psychological — can support adjustment and management of anxiety and depression.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mindfulness, acupuncture, and other complementary approaches
alternativeTreatments.description: Some patients report symptomatic benefit from mindfulness, acupuncture, or other complementary therapies. Evidence is limited; not curative but may support coping.

alternativeTreatments.name: Clinical trial enrollment
alternativeTreatments.description: Several investigational therapies including rituximab, cyclophosphamide, IVIG, antiviral agents, and metabolic modulators are in or have been in clinical trials. Patients should be considered for trial enrollment at specialist centres.

whySeeSpecialist: ME/CFS is best managed by primary care clinicians with chronic disease expertise, supported by specialist input from ME/CFS clinics, neurology, autonomic dysfunction services, and rehabilitation medicine for severe cases. Patients with severe orthostatic symptoms benefit from autonomic specialist evaluation. Patients with severe ME/CFS need home-based multidisciplinary care.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a clinician who recognizes ME/CFS as a biomedical illness, uses current diagnostic criteria, supports pacing-based management, and does not recommend graded exercise therapy. Avoid clinicians who promote ME/CFS as a primarily psychological condition. In the UK, NHS ME/CFS clinics are commissioned in many regions. In the US, specialist centres include the Bateman Horne Center, Stanford ME/CFS Initiative, and clinics at major academic centres.
hospitalCriteria: Specialist ME/CFS clinic or chronic complex disease service
Autonomic dysfunction service for POTS and orthostatic intolerance evaluation
Sleep medicine service for evaluation of unrefreshing sleep
Mental health services experienced in chronic disabling illness
keyFacilities: Bateman Horne Center, Stanford ME/CFS Initiative (US)
Open Medicine Foundation collaborating clinical centres
Autonomic dysfunction services (Vanderbilt, Mayo Clinic, etc.)
Specialist long COVID clinics integrating ME/CFS expertise
preventionStrategies: There is no proven way to prevent ME/CFS. Avoiding documented triggers (especially severe infectious mononucleosis and COVID-19 reinfection) may reduce risk in susceptible individuals.
Vaccinate against COVID-19, influenza, and other vaccine-preventable illnesses to reduce risk of post-infectious ME/CFS.
Manage acute viral illness with rest and avoid premature return to activity; pacing during recovery from any acute infection may reduce risk of post-viral fatigue.
Recognize and treat acute infections early (e.g., Lyme disease, EBV, Q fever).
Once diagnosed with ME/CFS, prevent worsening by adhering strictly to pacing and avoiding push-crash cycles.
Support family and friends in understanding ME/CFS to reduce social isolation and pressure to over-exert.
lifestyleModifications: Pace activities meticulously to stay within energy envelope; use heart rate monitors below anaerobic threshold.
Avoid 'push-crash' cycles — do less than you think you can on good days.
Maintain consistent sleep-wake schedule and good sleep hygiene.
Increase salt and fluid intake if orthostatic symptoms are present; use compression stockings.
Reduce sensory load — quieter environments, dimmer lighting, simpler social settings.
Engage in graded but cautious mental and physical activity only as tolerated, with substantial rest before fatigue accumulates.
Use accommodations at work or school (reduced hours, remote work, accessibility adaptations).
Connect with reputable patient communities and support organizations to reduce isolation.

recommended: Balanced Mediterranean-style diet with adequate protein and vegetables
Adequate hydration (2-3 L water per day) and salt intake (3-5 g daily) for orthostatic symptoms
Small, frequent meals to avoid post-prandial hypotension and gastrointestinal symptoms
Iron- and B12-rich foods if deficient on baseline testing
avoid: Excess caffeine (worsens sleep disturbance and orthostatic symptoms in some patients)
Alcohol (poorly tolerated in many ME/CFS patients)
High-glycemic carbohydrates causing energy spikes and crashes
Restrictive diets that worsen energy levels without medical guidance

exerciseGuidelines: Conventional graded exercise therapy is not recommended (NICE NG206, 2021/2024). Activity must be paced to stay within the individual's energy envelope and avoid post-exertional malaise. Movement that does not provoke PEM (e.g., gentle stretching, brief walking with rests) can be helpful. Heart-rate monitoring below the anaerobic threshold helps prevent crashes. Rehabilitation services with ME/CFS expertise can support individualized activity management.
dailyManagement: Maintain an activity and symptom diary to track triggers and patterns.
Use heart-rate monitor to stay below anaerobic threshold during activity.
Plan rest periods before fatigue accumulates.
Use compression stockings, salt, and water before upright activity if orthostatic symptoms present.
Connect regularly with healthcare team for symptom changes.
Access disability accommodations at work or school as needed.
prognosis: Outcome in ME/CFS is highly variable. Approximately 5-30% of patients recover fully or substantially over years; another 30-50% have meaningful improvement with consistent symptom management; the remainder have persistent or progressive disability. Recovery rates are higher in adolescents (around 40-50%), in post-EBV illness, and with early diagnosis and rigorous pacing. Severe and very severe ME/CFS (25% of all cases) tends to be more persistent, although individual cases of significant improvement are documented. Mortality from ME/CFS is low overall, but severe disease is associated with cardiovascular morbidity, suicide, and complications of immobility. Long COVID-related ME/CFS has been newly described and longer-term outcomes are being studied. Quality of life in ME/CFS is profoundly impaired — comparable in many studies to multiple sclerosis, chronic kidney disease on dialysis, or congestive heart failure. Access to disability accommodations, supportive family and social networks, and rigorous pacing are the most important modifiable factors for long-term wellbeing. Research into biomarkers, immunomodulators (rituximab failed in phase 3 but other agents are being investigated), antiviral and metabolic agents, and long COVID treatments is intensifying and offers cautious hope for future disease-modifying therapy.
recoveryTimeline: ME/CFS is a chronic condition without acute resolution. Recovery in the 5-30% who improve substantially typically occurs over 1-5 years. Pacing benefits become apparent over weeks to months. Symptomatic treatments (orthostatic medications, sleep aids, pain management) take days to weeks to show effect. Patients in long COVID-related ME/CFS may have somewhat different timelines being established by ongoing research.
complications: Severe functional disability with inability to work, attend school, or maintain social relationships.
Loss of income, housing instability, and financial hardship from long-term disability.
Severe depression and anxiety from chronic illness and isolation.
Suicide risk — elevated in chronic disabling illness and requires regular screening.
Cardiovascular complications from prolonged inactivity in severe disease.
Pressure injuries, contractures, and muscle atrophy in bed-bound patients.
Nutritional deficiency and weight loss in severe disease.
Comorbid conditions including POTS, fibromyalgia, IBS, migraine, anxiety, and depression.

supportResources.name: ME/CFS Foundation (US Solve ME)
supportResources.description: US-based patient organisation funding research and providing patient resources for ME/CFS.

supportResources.name: Action for ME (UK)
supportResources.description: Leading UK charity offering information, support, advocacy, and research funding for ME/CFS.

supportResources.name: ME Association (UK)
supportResources.description: UK patient organisation with medical advisory panel and helpline.

supportResources.name: Open Medicine Foundation
supportResources.description: International research organisation focused on ME/CFS, long COVID, and post-acute infection syndromes.

supportResources.name: Bateman Horne Center (US)
supportResources.description: Specialist clinical and research centre for ME/CFS, fibromyalgia, and post-COVID illness.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both produce widespread pain, fatigue, and sleep disturbance. Fibromyalgia is characterized primarily by widespread musculoskeletal pain and tender points; ME/CFS is characterized primarily by post-exertional malaise and disabling fatigue. Many patients meet criteria for both. Treatment overlaps substantially.

confusedWithConditions.name: Depression
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both can cause fatigue and reduced activity. Depression typically improves with rest and exercise; ME/CFS does NOT improve with rest and is severely worsened by exertion (post-exertional malaise). Depression has primary mood symptoms; ME/CFS has primary physical and cognitive symptoms with mood changes typically reactive to disability.

confusedWithConditions.name: Underactive Thyroid
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypothyroidism causes fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, and cognitive slowing — easily mistaken for ME/CFS. Distinguished by elevated TSH and low free T4; treatment with levothyroxine resolves symptoms. ME/CFS has normal thyroid function and is not improved by levothyroxine alone.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anxiety
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anxiety can cause physical symptoms (fatigue, racing thoughts, palpitations) that overlap with ME/CFS, particularly POTS-related symptoms. ME/CFS includes post-exertional malaise and cognitive impairment not present in primary anxiety. Anxiety frequently coexists with ME/CFS and requires separate management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IBS produces gastrointestinal symptoms (abdominal pain, bloating, altered bowel habit) without the disabling fatigue and PEM of ME/CFS. The two conditions frequently coexist and share central sensitization features.

relatedConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Closely related chronic illness with overlapping symptoms and frequent co-diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Depression
relatedConditions.relevance: Common comorbidity that requires separate management; does not cause ME/CFS but co-exists in 25-50%.

relatedConditions.name: Anxiety
relatedConditions.relevance: Common comorbidity, often related to autonomic dysfunction and chronic illness adjustment.

relatedConditions.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Common gastrointestinal comorbidity sharing central sensitization features.

relatedConditions.name: Underactive Thyroid
relatedConditions.relevance: Important differential — every ME/CFS workup includes TSH testing.

faqs.question: What is chronic fatigue syndrome?
faqs.answer: Chronic fatigue syndrome — formally myalgic encephalomyelitis / chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS) — is a chronic, complex, multisystem disease characterized by profound disabling fatigue, post-exertional malaise, unrefreshing sleep, and cognitive impairment or orthostatic intolerance. Symptoms must persist for more than 6 months and substantially impair function.

faqs.question: What is post-exertional malaise?
faqs.answer: Post-exertional malaise (PEM) is the worsening of symptoms 12-72 hours after physical, cognitive, or emotional exertion that would have been routine before illness. It can last hours to weeks and is the cardinal feature distinguishing ME/CFS from normal fatigue or deconditioning. Activities that previously caused no problem now provoke crashes lasting days.

faqs.question: Is ME/CFS the same as long COVID?
faqs.answer: Long COVID and ME/CFS overlap substantially. A subset of long COVID patients meets ME/CFS diagnostic criteria, with post-exertional malaise as the key shared feature. Long COVID has driven major expansion in ME/CFS prevalence since 2020. Many post-COVID patients meet IOM 2015 SEID criteria for ME/CFS.

faqs.question: Is ME/CFS a real disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. ME/CFS is recognized as a serious chronic biomedical illness by the World Health Organization, US Institute of Medicine, NIH, NICE, and major medical bodies. It is not 'just tiredness' and is not primarily psychological. Biomedical research identifies autonomic dysfunction, mitochondrial abnormalities, and immune dysregulation among other findings.

faqs.question: How is ME/CFS diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical using validated criteria (Institute of Medicine 2015 SEID criteria most commonly): substantial reduction in activity for over 6 months, post-exertional malaise, unrefreshing sleep, and either cognitive impairment or orthostatic intolerance. Blood tests rule out alternative explanations (thyroid disease, anemia, autoimmune disease). No diagnostic biomarker exists.

faqs.question: What causes ME/CFS?
faqs.answer: ME/CFS most often begins after an infection — particularly Epstein-Barr virus, SARS-CoV-2, or other viral illnesses (70-80% of cases). Underlying mechanisms include autonomic dysfunction (POTS, orthostatic intolerance), mitochondrial dysfunction, immune dysregulation, and central sensitization. Genetic susceptibility and stress contribute. The full pathophysiology remains incompletely understood.

faqs.question: Is ME/CFS curable?
faqs.answer: There is no cure for ME/CFS in 2026. Approximately 5-30% of patients recover fully or substantially over years; others have meaningful improvement with rigorous pacing and symptom management. Disease-modifying treatments are being investigated. Pacing, treatment of orthostatic intolerance and sleep disturbance, and symptomatic management form the foundation of care.

faqs.question: What is pacing?
faqs.answer: Pacing is the cornerstone management strategy for ME/CFS. It involves dividing activities into manageable segments, planning rest before fatigue accumulates, monitoring heart rate to stay below anaerobic threshold, and avoiding 'push-crash' cycles. Heart-rate monitors are increasingly used as objective tools. Pacing prevents post-exertional malaise rather than treating fatigue.

faqs.question: Is graded exercise therapy good for ME/CFS?
faqs.answer: No. The 2021 NICE NG206 guideline (updated 2024) explicitly removed graded exercise therapy as a recommended treatment because it can cause sustained worsening in ME/CFS. The earlier PACE trial that supported GET has been extensively criticized. Activity must be paced to stay within the energy envelope, not progressively increased on a fixed schedule.

faqs.question: How long does ME/CFS last?
faqs.answer: ME/CFS is a chronic condition with variable course. Approximately 5-30% recover fully or substantially over 1-5 years; others have persistent disability lasting years or decades. Recovery rates are higher in adolescents and in post-EBV illness. Long COVID-related ME/CFS outcomes are still being established.

faqs.question: Does exercise help ME/CFS?
faqs.answer: Conventional exercise programs typically worsen ME/CFS by provoking post-exertional malaise. Movement that does not provoke PEM (gentle stretching, brief walking with rests, very-low-intensity activity) can support general health. Exercise must be paced and individualized. Heart-rate monitoring prevents exceeding the anaerobic threshold.

faqs.question: Can ME/CFS be diagnosed in children?
faqs.answer: Yes. Pediatric ME/CFS affects 0.1-0.5% of children and adolescents. Diagnostic criteria are modified for younger patients (Carruthers Pediatric Diagnostic Criteria 2007). Educational and social disruption is profound. Recovery rates appear higher in adolescents (around 40-50% over years).

faqs.question: What drugs help with ME/CFS?
faqs.answer: No FDA-approved disease-modifying drug exists. Symptomatic medications include low-dose naltrexone for pain and immune modulation, pyridostigmine for orthostatic intolerance, midodrine and fludrocortisone for POTS, sleep aids, and pain management. SSRI/SNRI antidepressants help if depression is present. Many medications are used off-label.

faqs.question: Is POTS the same as ME/CFS?
faqs.answer: POTS (postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome) is a specific autonomic disorder; ME/CFS is a broader chronic illness. Up to 70% of ME/CFS patients have orthostatic intolerance, and many meet POTS criteria. POTS treatment (salt, fluids, midodrine, beta-blockers, ivabradine) is part of standard ME/CFS care for those with orthostatic symptoms.

faqs.question: Should I see a psychiatrist for ME/CFS?
faqs.answer: ME/CFS is not primarily a psychiatric condition, but depression and anxiety frequently coexist and benefit from separate treatment. Psychiatric care should focus on managing comorbid mental health conditions and supporting adjustment to chronic illness — not on the premise that ME/CFS itself is psychological. Choose a clinician who recognizes ME/CFS as a biomedical illness.

faqs.question: Can ME/CFS come back after recovery?
faqs.answer: Yes. Relapse after apparent recovery is common, often triggered by infection, surgery, severe stress, or over-exertion. Continued moderate pacing and protection from over-exertion are recommended even after substantial recovery. Vaccination against COVID-19 and influenza is encouraged to reduce relapse risk.

faqs.question: Is ME/CFS more common in women?
faqs.answer: Yes. Women are affected 2-4 times more often than men across studies. The reason is incompletely understood — possible contributions from sex differences in autonomic regulation, immune function, hormonal effects, and post-infectious response.

faqs.question: Does diet affect ME/CFS?
faqs.answer: No specific diet cures ME/CFS. A balanced Mediterranean-style diet with adequate hydration and salt (for orthostatic symptoms) is reasonable. Some patients identify food triggers; an elimination-and-rechallenge approach may help. Avoid restrictive diets without medical guidance — they can worsen energy levels and nutrition.

faqs.question: Can ME/CFS cause death?
faqs.answer: Direct mortality from ME/CFS is low, but severe disease is associated with elevated cardiovascular morbidity, complications of immobility, suicide risk in chronic disabling illness, and rarely complications of severe nutritional or fluid imbalance. Regular mental health screening and adequate support are essential.

faqs.question: Where can I find help with ME/CFS?
faqs.answer: Specialist ME/CFS clinics (NHS commissioned services in the UK, Bateman Horne Center and Stanford in the US, similar centres globally), reputable patient organizations (Solve ME, Action for ME, ME Association, Open Medicine Foundation), and clinical trial registries provide expert care and information. Avoid clinics that promote ME/CFS as psychological or recommend graded exercise therapy.

faqs.question: Will research find a cure for ME/CFS?
faqs.answer: Research has intensified substantially since the COVID-19 pandemic, with long COVID research catalysing biomedical investigation of ME/CFS. Ongoing trials evaluate immunomodulators, antiviral agents, mitochondrial modulators, and autonomic treatments. While no cure exists in 2026, the research pipeline is more robust than at any previous time.

metaTitle: Chronic Fatigue Syndrome: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: ME/CFS: post-exertional malaise, unrefreshing sleep, orthostatic intolerance. Diagnosis, pacing, and current symptomatic treatments medically reviewed.
